insights.insight: The ad industry pays $5–$150 CPA for probabilistic attribution that infers purchase intent. A cryptographically verified receipt eliminates the inference entirely — the purchase event is the signal, not a model of it.

With Chrome completing cookie deprecation and device fingerprinting under regulatory pressure, the cross-site tracking stack that most programmatic buying depends on has no clean successor — verified receipt data operates at a layer that never relied on any of it.

Amazon, Visa, Google, and Meta each see one dimension of the purchase funnel but none see the full transaction. Receipt-layer data sits below all four silos and captures what none of them can share with each other.

Verified receipt signals are settling at roughly $0.10 per confirmed purchase event against an industry CPA range of $5–$150. That gap is structural, not a pricing anomaly — it reflects the elimination of attribution overhead rather than a discount on the same product.

Consumer-submitted receipts enter the dataset whether or not the brand cooperates, meaning brands are being measured regardless. The strategic choice is between participating to shape that measurement or remaining blind to a dataset that already includes them.

The verified dataset has moved beyond digital and subscription purchases into physical retail — Home Depot and Starbucks receipts are now in the pipeline. Physical receipts are harder to fabricate and carry higher per-transaction value, which is relevant for CPG and brick-and-mortar advertisers evaluating data quality.
